name: Security
note: Security requirements are sourced from OWASP ASVS L1 (standards/asvs/level1.json). This file contains structural metadata only.

requirements.text: Credentials and secrets MUST NOT be hardcoded in source files
Sensitive data MUST NOT be logged (passwords, tokens, PII)
Sensitive data MUST be stored using platform-specific secure storage (Keychain, Vault)
Debug endpoints and verbose error details MUST be disabled in production builds
File permissions MUST follow least-privilege; world-readable config files are forbidden
Passwords MUST NOT appear as literals in source code
Security-sensitive configuration defaults MUST be secure out of the box
Sensitive information MUST NOT be exposed in error messages, stack traces, or system information disclosures
Sensitive data MUST be removed or masked before storage or transfer to less-trusted environments
Sensitive data MUST be transmitted only over encrypted channels; cleartext transmission of credentials is forbidden
Cryptographic algorithms MUST use adequate key lengths, strong ciphers, and cryptographically secure random number generators
Cookies containing sensitive data MUST set Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite attributes and MUST NOT use persistent storage for sensitive values
Directory listings MUST be disabled and files MUST NOT be accessible to unauthorized external parties
Sensitive data MUST NOT be passed in URL query strings via GET requests
Cryptographic algorithms and key management MUST use strong, current implementations
Sensitive data MUST NOT be stored in cleartext in files, cookies, memory, or environment variables
Sensitive information MUST NOT be exposed through source code, comments, error messages, or debug output
File and resource permissions MUST follow the principle of least privilege
Credentials MUST be protected during transport and storage
Resources MUST NOT be exposed to wrong spheres
All user input MUST be validated and sanitised before use
SQL queries MUST use parameterised statements; string concatenation is forbidden
HTML output MUST be escaped or rendered through a templating engine with auto-escaping
Deserialization of untrusted data MUST use safe parsers (JSON, not pickle/yaml.load)
Cryptographic algorithms MUST use current standards (AES-256, SHA-256+); MD5/SHA-1 are forbidden
XML parsers MUST disable external entity resolution
Application MUST NOT be susceptible to LDAP, code, or XPath injection; all dynamic queries MUST use parameterised or sanitised inputs
Special elements, input terminators, and invalid special element usage MUST be neutralised before processing
Output MUST be properly encoded or escaped, and alternate or Unicode encodings MUST be handled safely
File names and paths MUST NOT be controlled by external input; file uploads MUST restrict dangerous types and remote file inclusion MUST be prevented
Extra or unexpected parameters MUST be ignored or rejected, not silently processed
Data integrity MUST be verified using checksums or cryptographic signatures where applicable
Application MUST NOT include or execute self-replicating or malicious code patterns
URL redirects MUST validate target URLs against an allowlist to prevent open redirect attacks
Mass assignment MUST be prevented by explicitly whitelisting allowed fields for binding
Application MUST neutralize all injection vectors including command, expression, CRLF, header, and query injection
Software components MUST be verified for integrity, kept updated, and free from known vulnerabilities
Request and file interpretation MUST be consistent and resistant to smuggling or confusion attacks
Application design MUST follow secure design principles including defense in depth and fail-safe defaults
Input validation MUST use strict allowlists and verify boundary conditions
Authentication events (login, logout, failure) MUST be logged with timestamps
State-changing operations MUST be logged with actor identity
Log entries MUST NOT be modifiable by application-level code
Audit logs MUST include operation type, target resource, and outcome
Web functionality MUST NOT be included from untrusted third-party sources without integrity verification
Application MUST prevent clickjacking by restricting UI rendering in foreign frames (X-Frame-Options or CSP frame-ancestors)
Third-party component versions MUST be tracked and kept free of known vulnerabilities per OWASP Top Ten guidance
Application MUST protect against UI redress and clickjacking through untrusted web content inclusion
Authentication MUST be required for all non-public endpoints
Authorization checks MUST be enforced server-side, not client-side only
Session tokens MUST be invalidated on logout and password change
Rate limiting MUST be applied to authentication endpoints
Default credentials MUST NOT exist in any deployed configuration
Resource consumption MUST be bounded; rate limiting and allocation limits MUST prevent denial-of-service through resource exhaustion
Application MUST NOT include functionality from untrusted control spheres without explicit verification
Behavioral workflows MUST be enforced server-side to prevent step-skipping or out-of-order execution
Security-relevant events MUST be logged with neutralized output and sufficient detail
Security configuration MUST disable debug features, restrict cross-domain policies, and limit recursive entity expansion in production
Custom error pages MUST be used and MUST NOT reveal implementation details
Dynamic code evaluation (eval) MUST NOT be used with user-controlled input
Shell commands MUST NOT be constructed from user-controlled data
File paths MUST be validated against traversal attacks (../) before use
CORS policies MUST not use wildcard origins for authenticated endpoints
Dependencies MUST be pinned to exact versions and audited for known vulnerabilities
Server-side HTTP requests MUST validate target URLs against an allowlist
TLS certificates MUST be validated; certificate validation MUST NOT be disabled
Passwords MUST meet minimum complexity requirements and MUST NOT use long expiration without rotation; password changes MUST verify current credentials and recovery mechanisms MUST resist abuse
Authentication MUST include all critical steps; multi-factor or challenge-response flows MUST NOT be bypassable
Authorization checks MUST be enforced on every request; user-controlled keys MUST NOT allow access to other users' resources (IDOR)
Sessions MUST be protected against fixation attacks and cross-site request forgery (CSRF)
Application MUST NOT rely on reverse DNS resolution for security decisions
Primary communication channels MUST be protected; unprotected channels MUST NOT carry authentication data
Security controls MUST be enforced server-side; client-side enforcement alone is insufficient
HTTP method permissions MUST be validated server-side; trusting HTTP methods alone for access control is forbidden
Dangerous methods or functions MUST NOT be exposed to unauthorized callers
Application MUST NOT use broken or risky cryptographic algorithms for authentication or integrity verification
Authentication MUST resist spoofing, replay, certificate, and bypass attempts across all channels
Credentials MUST be unique, non-default, and sufficiently protected against disclosure
Authorization MUST be enforced server-side with proper privilege management and least-privilege access
